I'm now checking `jj help squash` for a more in-depth explanation and to confirm my understanding.\n\n\n","state":"done"},{"type":"tool-executeCommand","toolCallId":"GTXokKe7RXMz8E7o","state":"output-available","input":{"command":"cd /home/user/messy-project && jj help squash"},"output":{"output":"Move changes from a revision into another revision\r\n\r\nWithout any options, moves the changes from the working-copy revision to the parent revision.\r\n\r\nWith the `-r` option, moves the changes from the specified revision to the parent revision. Fails if\r\nthere are several parent revisions (i.e., the given revision is a merge).\r\n\r\nWith the `--from` and/or `--into` options, moves changes from/to the given revisions. If either is\r\nleft out, it defaults to the working-copy commit. For example, `jj squash --into @--` moves changes\r\nfrom the working-copy commit to the grandparent.\r\n\r\nIf, after moving changes out, the source revision is empty compared to its parent(s), and\r\n`--keep-emptied` is not set, it will be abandoned. Without `--interactive` or paths, the source\r\nrevision will always be empty.\r\n\r\nIf the source was abandoned and both the source and destination had a non-empty description, you\r\nwill be asked for the combined description. If either was empty, then the other one will be used.\r\n\r\nIf a working-copy commit gets abandoned, it will be given a new, empty commit. This is true in\r\ngeneral; it is not specific to this command.\r\n\r\nThe name \"squash\" comes from the idea of combining (squashing) the changes from multiple revisions\r\ntogether.\r\n\r\nEXPERIMENTAL FEATURES\r\n\r\nAn alternative squashing UI is available via the `-o`, `-A`, and `-B` options. Using any of these\r\noptions creates a new commit. They can be used together with one or more `--from` options (if no\r\n`--from` is specified, `--from @` is assumed).\r\n\r\nUsage: jj squash [OPTIONS] [FILESETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [FILESETS]...\r\n          Move only changes to these paths (instead of all paths)\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -r, --revision <REVSET>\r\n          Revision to squash into its parent (default: @). Incompatible with the experimental\r\n          `-o`/`-A`/`-B` options\r\n\r\n  -f, --from <REVSETS>\r\n          Revision(s) to squash from (default: @)\r\n\r\n  -t, --into <REVSET>\r\n          Revision to squash into (default: @)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --to]\r\n\r\n  -o, --onto <REVSETS>\r\n          (Experimental) The revision(s) to use as parent for the new commit (can be repeated to\r\n          create a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: -d, --destination]\r\n\r\n  -A, --insert-after <REVSETS>\r\n          (Experimental) The revision(s) to insert the new commit after (can be repeated to create a\r\n          merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --after]\r\n\r\n  -B, --insert-before <REVSETS>\r\n          (Experimental) The revision(s) to insert the new commit before (can be repeated to create\r\n          a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --before]\r\n\r\n  -m, --message <MESSAGE>\r\n          The description to use for squashed revision (don't open editor)\r\n\r\n  -u, --use-destination-message\r\n          Use the description of the destination revision and discard the description(s) of the\r\n          source revision(s)\r\n\r\n      --editor\r\n          Open an editor to edit the change description\r\n          \r\n          Forces an editor to open when using `--message` to allow the message to be edited\r\n          afterwards.\r\n\r\n  -i, --interactive\r\n          Interactively choose which parts to squash\r\n\r\n      --tool <NAME>\r\n          Specify diff editor to be used (implies --interactive)\r\n\r\n  -k, --keep-emptied\r\n          The source revision will not be abandoned\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. Edit the right side\r\nof the diff until it has the content you want in the first commit. Once you\r\nclose the editor, your revision will be split into two commits.\r\n\r\n[diff editor]:\r\n    https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/config/#editing-diffs\r\n\r\nBy default, the selected changes stay in the original commit, and the\r\nremaining changes go into a new child commit:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nL                 L'\r\n|                 |\r\nK (split)   =>    K\" (remaining)\r\n|                 |\r\nJ                 K' (selected)\r\n                  |\r\n                  J\r\n```\r\n\r\nWith `--parallel/-p`, the two parts become sibling commits instead of\r\nparent and child:\r\n\r\n```text\r\n                  L'\r\nL                / \\\r\n|               K'  |  (selected)\r\nK (split)  =>   |   K\" (remaining)\r\n|                \\ /\r\nJ                 J\r\n```\r\n\r\nWith `-o`, `-A`, or `-B`, the selected changes are extracted into a new\r\ncommit at the specified location, while the remaining changes stay in place:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nM                 M'\r\n|                 |\r\nL                 L'\r\n|                 |\r\nK (split)   =>    K' (remaining, stays here)\r\n|                 |\r\nJ                 J'\r\n                  |\r\n                  K\" (selected, inserted before J with -B J)\r\n```\r\n\r\nIf the change you split had a description, you will be asked to enter a\r\nchange description for each commit. If the change did not have a\r\ndescription, the second commit will not get a description, and you will be\r\nasked for a description only for the first commit.\r\n\r\nSplitting an empty commit is not supported because the same effect can be\r\nachieved with `jj new`.\r\n\r\nUsage: jj split [OPTIONS] [FILESETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [FILESETS]...\r\n          Files matching any of these filesets are put in the selected changes\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -i, --interactive\r\n          Interactively choose which parts to split\r\n          \r\n          This is the default if no filesets are provided.\r\n\r\n      --tool <NAME>\r\n          Specify diff editor to be used (implies --interactive)\r\n\r\n  -r, --revision <REVSET>\r\n          The revision to split\r\n          \r\n          [default: @]\r\n\r\n  -o, --onto <REVSETS>\r\n          The revision(s) to rebase the selected changes onto (can be repeated to create a merge\r\n          commit)\r\n          \r\n          Extracts the selected changes into a new commit based on the given revision(s). The\r\n          remaining changes stay in the original commit's location.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: -d, --destination]\r\n\r\n  -A, --insert-after <REVSETS>\r\n          The revision(s) to insert after (can be repeated to create a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          Extracts the selected changes into a new commit inserted after the given revision(s). The\r\n          remaining changes stay in the original commit's location.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --after]\r\n\r\n  -B, --insert-before <REVSETS>\r\n          The revision(s) to insert before (can be repeated to create a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          Extracts the selected changes into a new commit inserted before the given revision(s). The\r\n          remaining changes stay in the original commit's location.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --before]\r\n\r\n  -m, --message <MESSAGE>\r\n          The change description to use (don't open editor)\r\n          \r\n          Sets the description for the first commit (the one containing the selected changes). The\r\n          second commit keeps the original description.\r\n\r\n      --editor\r\n          Open an editor to edit the change description\r\n          \r\n          Forces an editor to open when using `--message` to allow the message to be edited\r\n          afterwards.\r\n\r\n  -p, --parallel\r\n          Split the revision into two parallel revisions instead of a parent and child\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check.
